humanities_role_explanation: Uses trivia questions and documents containing historical, literary, and artistic facts (e.g., WWII campaigns, novel characters, 1960s films) as factual QA items to evaluate general NLP models.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only reading comprehension benchmark consisting of written trivia questions and corresponding web/Wikipedia text documents.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Requires resolving complex question semantics, handling syntactic and lexical variations, and performing reasoning across multiple sentences in long documents to extract correct answer spans.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Designed as a general-purpose reading comprehension and question-answering benchmark for NLP systems.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: The paper was published in 2017 and evaluates pre-LLM neural models like BiDAF and linear entity classifiers.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Benchmarks older neural network architectures (BiDAF) and classifiers, not large language models.

arts_humanities_data.note: Features extensive coverage of Language and Linguistics, History, Literature and Literary Theory, and Visual Arts and Performing Arts via trivia questions.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates models using Exact Match (EM) and F1 score.

english_language.note: The paper, questions, and evidence documents are in English.

summary: The paper introduces TriviaQA, a large-scale reading comprehension dataset of over 650K question-answer-evidence triples. TriviaQA uses organically authored questions from trivia enthusiasts coupled with retrospectively collected Web and Wikipedia documents, creating a realistic and challenging benchmark.
key_findings: The best-performing baseline, a modified BiDAF model, achieves ~40% exact match under distant supervision on both Wikipedia and Web domains. This performance lags significantly behind the human baseline of approximately 80%, showing that the benchmark is highly challenging due to lexical variation and multi-sentence reasoning demands.
